reviewNotes: The U-M encoding is composite: its two alternate birth openings and shared Life continuation are followed without a new HEAD by the separately indexed Translation of Thomas Becket at Thiemke lines 2455-2528.
Thiemke's direct and mediated consultation routes are not conflated. He says he copied M, E, C, S, V, L, D, R, G, J, and B; H and W reached him through Horstmann 1887 and Black 1845 respectively.

The critical edition serializes both mutually exclusive birth redactions before their shared continuation. A reading path chooses either a or b; the XML's physical line count therefore exceeds the line count of either coherent version.
Manuscript dates, places, and dialects describe individual witnesses and are not silently inherited as composition facts about the anonymous work.

A complete local IIIF bundle contains every canvas published by the provider. Its work_portion maps the relevant leaves but does not imply that every medieval leaf survives or that the provider's digital sequence is physically exhaustive beyond its own completeness claim.
Local caching records research access and fixity, not permission to redistribute provider images. Rights statements remain scoped to named textual, image, catalog, or digital components.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a pinned copy of University of Michigan CME00142, which encodes Hermann Thiemke's 1919 critical edition of the South English Legendary Life of Thomas Becket and the discrete seventy-four-line Translation of Thomas Becket printed as lines 2455-2528. Thiemke based the b opening and shared continuation primarily on Bodleian MS Ashmole 43, supplied the mutually exclusive a opening from the Laud-family text printed by Horstmann, directly collated ten further witnesses, and used Black's 1845 Harley 2277 transcript as a second printed intermediary. No single witness supplies both alternative openings. The manifest separates this thirteen-witness editorial corpus from DIMEV's broader modern census: record 1507 lists sixteen Life witnesses, and record 4768 has fifteen active Translation occurrences in fourteen physical manuscripts because Rawlinson poet. 225 contains two copies. Complete public copies of all three editions are cached. Complete provider-exposed manuscript facsimiles are cached for Ashmole 43, Laud Misc. 108, Bodley 779, the Vernon Manuscript, CCCC MS 145, and Trinity R.3.25; exact work loci inside each complete codex are mapped. Laud Misc. 463 and Add. C. 220 have only non-overlapping selected images, while Lambeth MS 223 has selected images that reproduce the Translation boundary but not the whole Life. Exact catalogs, dated negative public-access findings, and reproduction routes are recorded for the remaining editorial and collateral witnesses.

catalogingSubject.label: Life and Translation of Saint Thomas of Canterbury
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the medieval Middle English Becket material represented by Hermann Thiemke's 1919 critical edition and encoded as CME00142. It contains the Life of Saint Thomas of Canterbury in two alternative opening redactions (Thiemke's birth-story versions a and b) followed by a shared vita and passio, plus the discrete seventy-four-line Translation of Thomas Becket (DIMEV 4768) at Thiemke lines 2455-2528. The two birth openings are alternative transmission states of the Life, not two complete independent lives. Thiemke's German headings, title page, and critical apparatus are modeled as editorial commentary rather than medieval work content. The unit is not the whole South English Legendary, the complete 1919 dissertation, or any one manuscript witness.

assertion.notes: Taylor gives approximately 1275 for the South English Legendary tradition. Thiemke's historical proposal of 1270 is retained as a related older argument, not false precision.

Laud Misc. 108 contains both the Life and Translation and is dated around 1280 by Taylor and 1280-1290 by Thiemke, while its current catalog gives a broader codex/core range. Harley MS 2277 is dated 1295-1305 and also contains both works. The normalized window preserves the attributed dating disagreement rather than silently preferring one cataloging tradition.

extent.description: Two medieval verse works are represented: the Life and the seventy-four-line Translation. The logical catalog extent follows one coherent Life reading path: 2,458 Life line elements plus 74 Translation lines, or 2,532 in total; four are Thiemke's alphabetic 2406a-d insertions beyond canonical numbering to 2528. The XML physically serializes both alternative birth openings and therefore has 2,742 running L elements plus six variant L elements inside notes. Four LG elements carry running text: two for birth version a, one for birth version b, and one shared container that begins with the common Life and continues across the line-2455 Translation boundary. Two further LG elements with six L elements are nested in apparatus notes. Its full structural count is 2 DIV1, 3 DIV2, 6 HEAD, 6 LG, 2,748 L, 2,699 NOTE, 186 PB, 464 MILESTONE, and 14 P elements, including two direct Anglo-Norman or mixed Latin-Anglo-Norman witness rubrics.

formStatements.description: Rhymed long-line couplets, conventionally described as septenary verse

scope.description: The two intrinsic medieval works are verse; prose occurs only in the separately modeled editorial framing and apparatus.

assertion.notes: Taylor argues that the legend was composed as a romance, disseminated as a saint's life, and read as history; the term does not reduce the work to a modern secular-romance category.
The poem adapts a source framed as history and incorporates legal documents into its narrative of church and crown.

notes: The exact line-2455 match to DIMEV 4768 establishes that CME00142 includes the Translation even though the XML does not introduce a new heading there.
The two birth-story versions are alternate Life openings. They must not be summed into a fictitious single medieval narrative.
Thiemke's direct-versus-mediated witness routes are preserved in the lineage manifest rather than flattened into thirteen direct consultations.
German editorial prose and apparatus do not change the medieval work-level form from verse or add German to the catalog summary's intrinsic language codes.
No manuscript date, dialect, image license, or damage statement has been transferred to the abstract composite work.

summaries.value: A long Middle English verse account of Thomas Becket's legendary parentage, youth, career as royal chancellor and archbishop of Canterbury, conflict with Henry II, exile, return, martyrdom, miracles, and posthumous cult. The accompanying Translation narrates the elevation and enshrinement of Becket's remains. The Life combines hagiography, romance, and historical writing: its foundational source is the Latin Quadrilogus, while its account of the Constitutions of Clarendon also draws on legal materials not present there.

CME00142 serializes Thiemke's 210-line birth-story version a, 156-line version b, a shared Life continuation represented by 2,302 running line elements, and the 74-line Translation at lines 2455-2528. A valid reading path chooses one birth version and yields 2,458 Life line elements, including four alphabetically numbered insertions at 2406a-d, followed by the 74-line Translation: 2,532 verse-line elements in total. The XML physically contains 2,742 running L elements because both alternative openings are serialized; six further L elements occur inside apparatus notes and are excluded from that running-text count.

Thiemke based the b opening and shared continuation primarily on Ashmole 43 (M), but the mutually exclusive a opening follows the Laud-family H text through Horstmann 1887; no witness supplies both openings. He copied and collated ten further manuscripts directly and reached Harley MS 2277 (W) through Black 1845. The lineage manifest keeps these scoped and mediated routes distinct. Modern DIMEV records 6687 and 1507 describe the Life's Laud and Harley redactions, while record 4768 identifies the discrete Translation embedded at the end of the encoded text and supplies a broader collateral witness census than Thiemke's editorial corpus.

Work, redaction, edition, witness, facsimile, encoding, and apparatus layers are separate. The two birth versions are alternative openings of one Life; the Translation is a second medieval work. German is confined to Thiemke's editorial layer. Manuscript dates, dialects, damage, and provider image licenses describe witnesses and surrogates, not the abstract medieval works.
